questions.stem: Two point charges of $+1\ \mu\text{C}$ each are placed $1\ \text{m}$ apart in vacuum. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the electric force between them?
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law gives $F = k\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(10^{-6})(10^{-6})}{1^2} = 9 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{N}$. The product of the two microcoulomb charges is $10^{-12}\ \text{C}^2$, and multiplying by $9 \times 10^{9}$ gives $9 \times 10^{-3}$. Forgetting the charges entirely leaves the bare constant $9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N}$. The trap is $9 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{N}$, which multiplies the charges but forgets to apply the constant $k$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges of $+2\ \mu\text{C}$ and $+3\ \mu\text{C}$ are separated by $0.30\ \text{m}$ in vacuum. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the force between them?
questions.explanation: Apply $F = k\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(3 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.30)^2}$. The numerator is $9 \times 10^{9} \times 6 \times 10^{-12} = 5.4 \times 10^{-2}$, and dividing by $0.09$ gives $0.60\ \text{N}$. Forgetting to square the distance gives $0.054/0.30 = 0.18\ \text{N}$. Using $r$ in centimetres scrambles the power of ten to give $6.0\ \text{N}$. The trap is $0.054\ \text{N}$, which is the numerator alone with the division by $r^2$ omitted.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: The force between two point charges is $F$ when they are a distance $r$ apart. If the distance is doubled to $2r$ while the charges are unchanged, what is the new force?
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law has $F \propto 1/r^2$, an inverse-square law. Doubling $r$ multiplies $r^2$ by $4$, so the force drops to $F/4$. Treating the dependence as $1/r$ would give $F/2$, which ignores the square. Forces that grow with distance ($2F$ or $4F$) contradict the inverse relationship entirely. The trap is $F/2$, which forgets that it is $r^2$, not $r$, in the denominator.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two equal point charges experience a force $F$ between them. If both charges are doubled and the separation is kept the same, what is the new force?
questions.explanation: The force is proportional to the product $q_1 q_2$. Doubling each charge multiplies the product by $2 \times 2 = 4$, so the new force is $4F$. Doubling only one charge would give $2F$, but here both change. There is no eighth power involved, so $8F$ is wrong. The trap is $2F$, which doubles the product once instead of accounting for both charges doubling.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges of $+4\ \mu\text{C}$ and $+9\ \mu\text{C}$ sit $0.60\ \text{m}$ apart in vacuum. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the force on the $4\ \mu\text{C}$ charge?
questions.explanation: By Newton's third law each charge feels the same magnitude: $F = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-6})(9 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.60)^2}$. The numerator is $9 \times 10^{9} \times 36 \times 10^{-12} = 0.324$, and dividing by $0.36$ gives $0.90\ \text{N}$. Forgetting to square the distance gives $0.324/0.60 = 0.54\ \text{N}$. The value $0.45\ \text{N}$ halves the answer as if only one charge felt the force. The trap is $0.54\ \text{N}$, which omits the square on the separation.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges of $+3\ \mu\text{C}$ each are placed $0.10\ \text{m}$ apart in vacuum. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the force between them?
questions.explanation: Compute $F = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(3 \times 10^{-6})^2}{(0.10)^2} = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-12}}{0.01}$. The numerator is $9 \times 10^{9} \times 9 \times 10^{-12} = 0.081$, and dividing by $0.01$ gives $8.1\ \text{N}$. Forgetting the square on the distance gives $0.081/0.10 = 0.81\ \text{N}$. Mishandling the power of ten on $r$ gives $81\ \text{N}$. The trap is $0.81\ \text{N}$, which leaves the separation unsquared.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges attract each other with a force of $0.36\ \text{N}$ when $0.20\ \text{m}$ apart. If they are moved to $0.10\ \text{m}$ apart, what is the new force?
questions.explanation: Halving the separation multiplies the inverse-square force by $2^2 = 4$, so $F_{\text{new}} = 4 \times 0.36 = 1.44\ \text{N}$. The distance went down, so the force must go up, ruling out the smaller values $0.18\ \text{N}$ and $0.09\ \text{N}$. Multiplying by only $2$ instead of $4$ gives $0.72\ \text{N}$. The trap is $0.72\ \text{N}$, which uses $1/r$ scaling rather than the $1/r^2$ of Coulomb's law.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges of $+12\ \mu\text{C}$ and $+3\ \mu\text{C}$ are $0.30\ \text{m}$ apart in vacuum. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the force between them?
questions.explanation: Use $F = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(12 \times 10^{-6})(3 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.30)^2}$. The numerator is $9 \times 10^{9} \times 36 \times 10^{-12} = 0.324$, and dividing by $0.09$ gives $3.6\ \text{N}$. Leaving the separation unsquared gives $0.324/0.30 = 1.08 \approx 1.2\ \text{N}$. The value $0.36\ \text{N}$ slips a power of ten. The trap is $1.2\ \text{N}$, which divides by $r$ rather than $r^2$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two protons are held $1.0 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{m}$ apart, roughly the spacing in a nucleus. Taking the proton charge as $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ and $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the electric force between them?
questions.explanation: Compute $F = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{(1.0 \times 10^{-15})^2}$. The charge squared is $2.56 \times 10^{-38}$, and the distance squared is $10^{-30}$, so $F = 9 \times 10^{9} \times 2.56 \times 10^{-38}/10^{-30} = 9 \times 10^{9} \times 2.56 \times 10^{-8} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{2}\ \text{N}$. This large force on tiny particles is why a strong nuclear force is needed to hold nuclei together. Leaving the distance unsquared gives the much smaller $1.4 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{N}$. The trap is $2.3 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{N}$, which mishandles the exponent arithmetic by a factor of $10^{6}$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charge $q_1 = +6\ \mu\text{C}$ at the origin and a charge $q_2 = -6\ \mu\text{C}$ at $x = 0.20\ \text{m}$ are placed on a line. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the nature and magnitude of the force on $q_2$?
questions.explanation: Opposite charges attract, so $q_2$ is pulled toward $q_1$. The magnitude is $F = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(6 \times 10^{-6})^2}{(0.20)^2} = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{36 \times 10^{-12}}{0.04} = 8.1\ \text{N}$. The sign of the charges sets the direction, not the magnitude formula, so the force is attractive, not repulsive. Halving gives the spurious $4.05\ \text{N}$, and forgetting to square $r$ gives $0.81\ \text{N}$. The trap is the repulsive option, which has the right size but mistakes unlike charges for like ones.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Three point charges lie on a straight line: $q_1 = +5\ \mu\text{C}$ at $x = 0$, a charge $q_3 = +2\ \mu\text{C}$ at $x = 0.10\ \text{m}$, and $q_2 = +5\ \mu\text{C}$ at $x = 0.30\ \text{m}$. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude and direction of the net force on $q_3$?
questions.explanation: From $q_1$ (at distance $0.10\ \text{m}$): $F_1 = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.10)^2} = 9.0\ \text{N}$, pushing $q_3$ in the $+x$ direction. From $q_2$ (at distance $0.20\ \text{m}$): $F_2 = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.20)^2} = 2.25\ \text{N}$, pushing $q_3$ in the $-x$ direction. The net is $9.0 - 2.25 = 6.75\ \text{N}$ toward $+x$. Adding the two instead of subtracting gives $11.25\ \text{N}$. The trap is $2.25\ \text{N}$, which keeps only the smaller force and forgets the dominant push from $q_1$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charge $q_1 = +1\ \mu\text{C}$ is at $x = 0$ and a charge $q_2 = +4\ \mu\text{C}$ is at $x = 0.30\ \text{m}$. At what point on the line between them is the net electric force on a small test charge zero?
questions.explanation: The forces balance where $\dfrac{q_1}{x^2} = \dfrac{q_2}{(0.30 - x)^2}$, that is $\dfrac{1}{x^2} = \dfrac{4}{(0.30 - x)^2}$. Taking square roots, $\dfrac{0.30 - x}{x} = 2$, so $0.30 - x = 2x$ and $x = 0.10\ \text{m}$. The null point sits closer to the smaller charge, as expected. The midpoint $0.15\ \text{m}$ ignores the unequal charges, and $0.20\ \text{m}$ puts it nearer the larger charge, which is backwards. The trap is $0.20\ \text{m}$, which solves the ratio in the wrong direction and lands on the side of the bigger charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two identical conducting spheres carry charges of $+8\ \mu\text{C}$ and $-2\ \mu\text{C}$ and attract each other with a force $F$ when $0.20\ \text{m}$ apart. They are touched together and returned to the same separation. What is the new force, and is it attractive or repulsive?
questions.explanation: Before contact the product of charges has magnitude $8 \times 2 = 16\ \mu\text{C}^2$ and the force is attractive. After contact the spheres share the net charge $(+8 - 2)/2 = +3\ \mu\text{C}$ each, so both are positive and now repel. The new product is $3 \times 3 = 9\ \mu\text{C}^2$, so the magnitude becomes $\dfrac{9}{16}F$ at the same separation. The sign of the force flips from attractive to repulsive because both spheres end up positive. The trap is the attractive $\tfrac{9}{16}F$ option, which gets the magnitude right but forgets that two like charges repel.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Coulomb's law for the force between point charges in vacuum is written $F = k\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$, where $k = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$. What does the symbol $\varepsilon_0$ represent?
options: The dielectric constant of the charges
questions.explanation: The constant $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space, with value about $8.85 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C}^2/(\text{N m}^2)$, and it sets the strength of the electric interaction in vacuum. Permeability $\mu_0$ is its magnetic counterpart, a different constant. The electron charge is $e$, a separate quantity. A dielectric constant is a property of a material, not of the charges. The trap is permeability, which is easy to confuse with permittivity but belongs to magnetism.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two equal positive point charges $+Q$ are fixed at two corners of an equilateral triangle of side $0.30\ \text{m}$. A third charge $+1\ \mu\text{C}$ at the remaining corner feels a force of magnitude $0.10\ \text{N}$ from each of the two fixed charges. What is the magnitude of the net force on the third charge?
questions.explanation: The two forces have equal magnitude $0.10\ \text{N}$ and, by the geometry of the equilateral triangle, the angle between them is $60^\circ$. The resultant of two equal vectors of size $f$ at $60^\circ$ is $R = \sqrt{f^2 + f^2 + 2f^2\cos 60^\circ} = f\sqrt{3}$, so $R = 0.10\sqrt{3} \approx 0.17\ \text{N}$. Adding the magnitudes as if they were parallel gives $0.20\ \text{N}$, and a $90^\circ$ assumption gives $0.10\sqrt{2}\ \text{N}$. The trap is $0.20\ \text{N}$, which treats the two forces as pointing the same way instead of $60^\circ$ apart.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: When the medium between two charges is changed from vacuum to a material with relative permittivity (dielectric constant) $\varepsilon_r = 4$, how does the electric force between two fixed charges at fixed separation change?
options: It becomes one quarter of its vacuum value
It becomes one half of its vacuum value
It becomes four times its vacuum value
questions.explanation: In a dielectric the Coulomb force is reduced by the factor $\varepsilon_r$: $F = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$. With $\varepsilon_r = 4$ the force is divided by $4$, becoming a quarter of the vacuum value. The medium screens the charges rather than strengthening their interaction, so the force decreases, not increases. It certainly does change, ruling out the unchanged option. The trap is the one-half option, which halves rather than quarters, as if the factor entered as $\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges repel each other. From this observation alone, what can be concluded about the signs of the charges?
options: They have the same sign, either both positive or both negative
questions.explanation: Like charges repel and unlike charges attract. A repulsive force tells us the charges are alike in sign, but the rule works the same whether both are positive or both are negative, so we cannot pin down which. Concluding both positive, or both negative, claims more than the evidence allows. Opposite signs would produce attraction, the reverse of what is seen. The trap is the opposite-signs option, which mixes up the attraction and repulsion rules.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges of $+1\ \mu\text{C}$ each are placed $3.0\ \text{cm}$ apart in vacuum. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the force between them?
questions.explanation: Convert the separation to metres: $3.0\ \text{cm} = 0.030\ \text{m}$. Then $F = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(10^{-6})^2}{(0.030)^2} = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{10^{-12}}{9 \times 10^{-4}} = 10\ \text{N}$. Leaving the distance in centimetres ($r^2 = 9$) gives the badly wrong $10^{-3}\ \text{N}$ scaled up, near $3 \times 10^{5}$. Using $r = 0.30\ \text{m}$ by misplacing the decimal gives $0.10\ \text{N}$. The trap is $0.10\ \text{N}$, which slips the separation by a factor of ten and so the force by a factor of one hundred.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A small charged sphere of mass $2.0\ \text{g}$ carrying charge $+q$ hangs in equilibrium directly above a fixed charge $+Q$, held up purely by the electric repulsion. Taking $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$, what is the magnitude of the electric force on the hanging sphere?
questions.explanation: For the sphere to float, the upward electric force must balance its weight: $F = mg = (2.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg})(10\ \text{m/s}^2) = 0.020\ \text{N}$. The key step is converting $2.0\ \text{g}$ to $2.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg}$. Forgetting the gram-to-kilogram conversion gives $20\ \text{N}$ or, with other slips, $0.20\ \text{N}$. The value $0.0020\ \text{N}$ divides by $g$ instead of multiplying. The trap is $0.20\ \text{N}$, which keeps the mass off by a factor of ten in the unit conversion.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: How does the inverse-square form of Coulomb's law, $F \propto 1/r^2$, compare with Newton's law of gravitation between two masses?
options: Both are inverse-square laws in the separation, but Coulomb's force can be attractive or repulsive while gravity is only attractive.
Coulomb's law is inverse-square but gravity is inverse-distance.
Both forces depend on $1/r$ rather than $1/r^2$.
Both are inverse-square laws and both can be attractive or repulsive.
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law and Newton's gravitation share the same $1/r^2$ distance dependence and the same product-of-sources structure. They differ in sign behaviour: electric charges come in two signs, so the force can attract or repel, whereas mass is always positive and gravity only ever attracts. Gravity is not an inverse-distance law, so that option is wrong, and both are genuinely inverse-square, not $1/r$. The trap is the option claiming gravity can also repel, which is false since there is no negative mass.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges of $+2\ \mu\text{C}$ and $+3\ \mu\text{C}$ are $0.30\ \text{m}$ apart in vacuum. Taking $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, find the force between them and name the law used.
questions.solution: The force is $F = k\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(3 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.30)^2} = $ {{1}} $\text{ N}$. This calculation uses {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The numerator is $9 \times 10^{9} \times 6 \times 10^{-12} = 0.054$, and dividing by $0.09$ gives $0.6\ \text{N}$. The force between point charges is governed by Coulomb's law.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two point charges attract with a force of $0.36\ \text{N}$ when $0.20\ \text{m}$ apart. They are moved to $0.10\ \text{m}$ apart with the charges unchanged. Find the new force.
questions.solution: Halving the distance changes the inverse-square force by a factor of {{1}}, so the new force is $0.36 \times $ that factor $ = $ {{2}} $\text{ N}$.
questions.explanation: Since $F \propto 1/r^2$, halving $r$ multiplies the force by $2^2 = 4$, giving $0.36 \times 4 = 1.44\ \text{N}$. A smaller separation means a stronger force.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charge $q_1 = +1\ \mu\text{C}$ sits at $x = 0$ and a charge $q_2 = +4\ \mu\text{C}$ sits at $x = 0.30\ \text{m}$. Find the point between them where the net force on a test charge is zero.
questions.solution: Setting the forces equal gives $\dfrac{1}{x^2} = \dfrac{4}{(0.30 - x)^2}$, so $\dfrac{0.30 - x}{x} = 2$. Solving, $x = $ {{1}} $\text{ m}$. The null point lies nearer the {{2}} charge.
questions.explanation: From $0.30 - x = 2x$ we get $3x = 0.30$, so $x = 0.10\ \text{m}$. The balance point is always closer to the weaker (smaller) charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Three point charges lie on the $x$-axis: $q_1 = +5\ \mu\text{C}$ at $x = 0$, $q_3 = +2\ \mu\text{C}$ at $x = 0.10\ \text{m}$, and $q_2 = +5\ \mu\text{C}$ at $x = 0.30\ \text{m}$. Take $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$. Find the net force on $q_3$.
questions.solution: From $q_1$ at $0.10\ \text{m}$: $F_1 = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.10)^2} = $ {{1}} $\text{ N}$ toward $+x$. From $q_2$ at $0.20\ \text{m}$: $F_2 = 2.25\ \text{N}$ toward $-x$. The net force is $F_1 - F_2 = $ {{2}} $\text{ N}$ in the $+x$ direction.
questions.explanation: The nearer charge $q_1$ pushes with $9.0\ \text{N}$ and the farther $q_2$ pushes back with $2.25\ \text{N}$, so the net is $9.0 - 2.25 = 6.75\ \text{N}$ in the $+x$ direction. Forces from like charges along a line subtract when they oppose.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two protons are $1.0 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{m}$ apart. Take the proton charge as $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ and $k = 9 \times 10^{9}\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$. Find the magnitude of the electric force and state whether it is attractive or repulsive.
questions.solution: The force magnitude is $F = 9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{(1.0 \times 10^{-15})^2} \approx $ {{1}} $\times 10^{2}\ \text{N}$. Since both charges are positive, the force is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The charge squared is $2.56 \times 10^{-38}$ and the distance squared is $10^{-30}$, giving $F = 9 \times 10^{9} \times 2.56 \times 10^{-8} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{2}\ \text{N}$. Two protons carry like charge, so they repel.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two charges interact with force $F$ in vacuum. They are then immersed in a dielectric medium of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 4$ at the same separation. Find the factor by which the force changes and the SI unit of force.
questions.solution: In a dielectric the force becomes $F/\varepsilon_r$, so the force is multiplied by the factor {{1}} (give it as a decimal). Force is measured in the SI unit called the {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Dividing by $\varepsilon_r = 4$ multiplies the force by $1/4 = 0.25$. The medium screens the charges, weakening the force. Force is measured in newtons.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers
